title: Dyeing with blackberries
subtitle: Extracting pink, purple, and grey tones from blackberry juice on mordanted wool and cotton
excerpt: Blackberries (Rubus fruticosus) produce anthocyanin pigments that dye fibre in the pink-to-purple range in an acidic bath and shift toward grey-green in alkaline conditions. The colour is lively but has modest light-fastness: expect pink and purple tones to fade toward grey-brown within months of direct sunlight. Mordanting with alum extends the dye's life on the fibre. This guide covers extraction and the pH shift technique.
sourceNotes: Blackberry as a natural dye source and anthocyanin pH behaviour are documented in contemporary natural dyeing guides. Method is observation-led and consistent with workshop instruction.

content.text: that behave differently depending on the pH of the dye bath. The

technique uses this property to produce a range of related tones from a single dye bath. Note that all anthocyanin dyes have limited light-fastness and are best used for items not exposed to prolonged direct sunlight.

items.name: Washing soda (for alkaline pH shift, optional)
Stainless steel or enamel dye pot (dedicated)

content.text: Crush the blackberries by hand or with a potato masher and add them to the dye pot with enough cold water to cover. Simmer for 20-30 minutes until the liquid is deeply coloured. Do not boil hard: high temperatures degrade anthocyanins more quickly. Strain out the fruit pulp through muslin or a fine sieve and return the liquid to the pot. Add 50-100 ml of white wine vinegar to acidify the bath.
Step 2: Dye in the acidic bath

Add the pre-wetted mordanted fibre to the warm bath. Raise to 70-75 degrees Celsius over 20 minutes and hold for 30-40 minutes, stirring gently. The acidic vinegar bath produces pink-purple tones. Keep the temperature below 80 degrees: higher temperatures break down anthocyanins faster and the colour loses saturation.

To see the pH shift effect, remove a small skein of freshly dyed pink-purple wool and add half a teaspoon of washing soda dissolved in water to that portion of the bath. The colour will shift toward grey-purple or greyish-green within minutes. This shift is reversible: adding vinegar back will return the pink tone. Use the shifted colour if you prefer the muted grey-purple result.

Remove the fibre and let it cool in the pot before rinsing. Rinse in cool water with a small amount of vinegar added to the final rinse to stabilise the pink-purple tone. Dry away from direct sunlight. Store dyed items out of prolonged light to extend the colour life.

items.fix: Blackberry dye has inherently limited light-fastness. Use it for items stored away from sunlight (scarves worn occasionally, decorative pieces). Mordanting with alum delays but does not prevent fading.
items.cause: Normal behaviour for anthocyanin dyes on light-exposed items
items.problem: The colour fades to a dull grey-brown within a few weeks

items.fix: Keep the extraction temperature below 80 degrees. Ensure the wool is alum-mordanted. Unmordanted wool will take very little anthocyanin colour.
items.cause: Berries over-simmered at high heat, or wool not mordanted before dyeing
items.problem: The dyed colour is much weaker than expected

items.fix: Use a stainless steel or enamel pot. If your tap water is hard or iron-rich, add extra vinegar to the bath to counteract the alkalinity and reduce iron interaction.
items.cause: Iron contamination from the pot or tap water reacting with the anthocyanin
items.problem: The colour is already grey rather than pink-purple
